evidence: org.jspecify @Nullable is used consistently on return types that can be null: BlackLabSnippetParser.extractLemmaFromMatch, extractPosFromMatch, extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition, extractHeadword, extractPlainTextTokenAt; HttpApiUtils.requireParam; ExplorationHandlers.resolveRelationConfig; ExplorationHandlers.resolveExploreParams
QueryExecutor interface uses Closeable and declares checked IOException on all methods — type contracts align with implementation

ExplorationResult.empty(String seed) Javadoc states '@param seed the seed word; must not be null' but the calling code in SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern passes null explicitly: `if (seed == null || seed.isEmpty()) { return ExplorationResult.empty(seed); }` — contract violated at the call site
GrammarConfig.getConfigPath() is annotated @Nullable, consistent with the constructor accepting null for configPath when parsing from a Reader
RelationConfig uses Optional<RelationType> for the relationType field, and callers consistently check isEmpty()/orElseThrow() before use

CollocateQueryHelper constructor receives BlackLabIndex as a parameter with no null annotation; in tests it is passed null and the field is used in methods that are overridden — an invisible null-safety contract

SketchHandlers.handleRelationsForType calls rel.relationType().orElseThrow() inside a branch guarded by rel.relationType().orElse(null) == relationType — the Optional type does not capture the now-guaranteed non-empty state, so orElseThrow carries a misleading failure semantics
Map<String, Object> used extensively for HTTP response building — no typed response DTOs; type information is entirely lost at the serialization boundary

strengths: @Nullable annotations from org.jspecify applied consistently on all public methods with nullable returns in BlackLabSnippetParser and HttpApiUtils
Optional<RelationType> used for the relationType relation config field, with consistent isEmpty()/orElseThrow() call patterns at use sites
QueryExecutor interface declares all exceptions (IOException), giving callers reliable type-level failure contracts
GrammarConfig.getConfigPath() correctly annotated @Nullable matching the constructor semantics
type_safety.issue_character: Issues are concentrated around two patterns: a null contract violation in ExplorationResult.empty() where the documented precondition is immediately broken by the only caller passing null, and untyped Map<String,Object> response objects that erase type safety at the serialization boundary.
type_safety.score_rationale: The codebase applies Java's available nullability tooling (jspecify @Nullable, Optional) consistently across the core API. The main deduction comes from the ExplorationResult.empty(null) contract breach, which is a correctness concern, and the pervasive use of Map<String,Object> for response construction that sacrifices type safety at the HTTP boundary. These are meaningful gaps but not systemic — most of the type surface is well-annotated.

issues.summary: ExplorationResult.empty() documents 'must not be null' but its only call site passes null
evidence: ExplorationResult.empty Javadoc: '@param seed the seed word; must not be null'
SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern: `if (seed == null || seed.isEmpty()) { return ExplorationResult.empty(seed); }` — passes null when seed is null
issues.suggestion: Either annotate the empty() parameter with @Nullable and remove the 'must not be null' constraint, or add a null check inside empty() that substitutes an empty string, and update exploreByPattern to pass an empty string instead of null

issues.summary: CollocateQueryHelper constructor accepts BlackLabIndex without @Nullable, but tests pass null
evidence: Test stub: `new CollocateQueryHelper(null) { @Override long getTotalFrequency(...) { ... } }` — relies on overriding methods that use the field
issues.suggestion: Add @Nullable to the BlackLabIndex constructor parameter, or split into a test-only subclass / add a package-private constructor that does not require an index; annotating the field helps static analysis tools warn if a non-overriding method attempts to use it

root_causes: Null-contract drift: ExplorationResult.empty() Javadoc precondition was written with non-null intent but the calling pattern requires null-tolerance
Untyped response maps: HTTP response DTOs were never formalized, leaving Map<String,Object> as the de-facto response type across the API layer
